title: In My Place Condemned He Stood
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-04-24 -AM- In My Place Condemned He Stood - 1 Peter 2_21-25 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Going Back to the Foundation
subpoints: The sermon goes back to 1 Peter 2:21-25 after preaching 3:7 the previous week
Peter's letter is full of commands; these commands are always grounded in what Christ has done
The danger of expository preaching: going week after week on commands can put distance between duty and gospel
Purpose: to anchor all Christian obedience in the work of Christ

outline.title: 1. The Call — and Why Christ Is the Reason for Everything
subpoints: 'For this you were called' — effectual calling by the Holy Spirit through the gospel
Like the dry bones of Ezekiel 37 — we were dead, and God called us to life through the preaching of His Word
Every command in the Christian life — marriage, parenting, citizenship, church — flows from this single foundation: Christ suffered, died, and rose again for us
The unchanging nature of Christ's work frees us to love others regardless of what they do

outline.title: 2. Christ Suffered for Us
subpoints: 'Suffered' is broader than 'died' — it includes all the humiliation of Christ's earthly life (WSC Q.27)
He was unjustly accused, betrayed by friends, mocked, persecuted — while never retaliating
'Who when He was reviled, did not revile in return; when He suffered, He did not threaten'
He entrusted all judgment to the Father — illustrated by Gethsemane: 'not my will but Yours be done'
Peter's language saturated with Isaiah 53: He bore our grief, carried our sorrows, was wounded for our transgressions

outline.title: 3. He Bore Our Sins in His Body on the Tree
subpoints: 'Who Himself bore our sins in His own body on the tree' — substitutionary atonement
Philip Bliss: 'Bearing shame and scoffing rude, in my place condemned He stood'
Christ received what we deserved; we receive what we don't deserve
By His stripes we are healed — not temporal healing but spiritual wholeness: justification, peace with God
Calvin: 'When we behold the Son of God... we need to reckon afresh that it is ourselves we gaze upon, for He stood in our place'

outline.title: 4. He Did It Willingly
subpoints: John 10:18: 'No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of myself'
John 13: the night before the crucifixion, He looked at the disciples fighting about greatness — and loved them
He did not resist because He knew that if He did, He could not save us
The suffering was not easy because He was God — the blood-drops in Gethsemane prove otherwise

outline.title: Application
subpoints: If you are in Christ: rejoice — the price of your soul has been paid in full
Live righteously in response to what Christ has done, not in order to earn what He has already given
Live happily — God's face shines upon you because He frowned upon His Son
If you are not in Christ: come to Him today; He will not turn away anyone who comes to Him

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro returns to 1 Peter 2:21-25 (after preaching 3:7 the previous week) to anchor the entire section of commands in the work of Christ. He argues that every command in the Christian life flows from the one foundation: Christ suffered, died, and rose again for us in our place. Drawing heavily from Isaiah 53, he expounds the substitutionary nature of Christ's suffering, His willingness to bear what we deserved, and the peace with God that believers enjoy because of His work — closing with applications to both believers (rejoice, live righteously) and the unbeliever.
transcript:
## Introduction: Going Back to the Foundation

You will notice that we went backward in our reading. Last week I preached from 1 Peter chapter 3, verse 7, and now we are back in chapter 2. There is a reason for that.

We are in a section of 1 Peter full of commands — imperatives. Peter is telling us what we are to do: as citizens, as employees, as husbands, as wives. All the way from chapter 2, verse 13 to the end of the letter, the emphasis is on duty, on submission, on self-denial.

These commands are good and glorious. But the nature of expository preaching is such that going verse by verse through a long section of commands can put distance between what God requires of us and what God has done for us — and that distance is not natural to the text. Peter never strays far from the work of Christ.

So before going to the next section of commands, I want us to go back to verses 21 through 25 and look again at the work of Christ — the reason why we can obey, the reason why we are able to live the way Peter calls us to live.

## 1. The Call — and Why Christ Is the Reason for Everything

> "For this you were called, because Christ also suffered for us..." (1 Peter 2:21)

*For this you were called.* The Holy Spirit laid hold of your heart and changed it so that you could believe — and now you want to believe and follow the Lord Jesus Christ. What happened to the valley of dry bones in Ezekiel 37 happened to you. Dead. Dry. Not mostly dead — *dry bones*. And then God called, and life came.

This is the foundation of everything. Not just of Peter's commands — of everything. Why does your marriage matter? Because Christ died for you. Why does your parenting matter? Because Christ died for you. Why does your citizenship, your membership in this congregation, your investment in one another matter? Because Christ died for you.

And that fact does not change regardless of what other people do. The unchanging nature of what Christ has done for us frees us to love others without having to evaluate whether they are worthy of our love — because *He* loved us when we were not worthy.

## 2. Christ Suffered for Us

> "Because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example..." (1 Peter 2:21)

Notice: Peter says *suffered*, not *died*. This is intentional. The cross was the culmination of our Savior's suffering, but not the entirety of it. Our Shorter Catechism asks in question 27, "Wherein did Christ's humiliation consist?" The answer: in His low condition at birth, in being made under the law, in undergoing the miseries of this life, in the wrath of God, and in the cursed death of the cross, in burial, and in continuing under the power of death.

He was unjustly accused. He was betrayed by the men He had loved most. He was mocked and persecuted — while never retaliating. He was lied about. All the while:

> "Who, when He was reviled, did not revile in return; when He suffered, He did not threaten, but committed Himself to Him who judges righteously." (1 Peter 2:23)

He entrusted all of it to His Father. Remember Gethsemane — sweating blood, asking for the cup to pass, and then: *not my will, but Yours be done.* He committed Himself.

Peter's language throughout these verses is saturated with Isaiah 53. He bore our grief. He carried our sorrows. He was wounded for our transgressions, bruised for our iniquities. The chastisement for our peace was upon Him. All our iniquities were laid upon Him.

## 3. He Bore Our Sins in His Body on the Tree

> "Who Himself bore our sins in His own body on the tree, that we, having died to sins, might live for righteousness — by whose stripes you were healed." (1 Peter 2:24)

Philip Bliss captured it: *Bearing shame and scoffing rude, in my place condemned He stood. Sealed my pardon with His blood. Hallelujah! What a Savior!*

This is what is at stake in Christ's suffering. Not merely an inspiring example. Not merely a demonstration of divine love. Your salvation. Your soul. The guilt and penalty of your sin laid upon an innocent substitute — so that you did not have to bear it yourself.

He received what we deserved. We receive what we don't deserve. By His stripes we are healed — not of physical sickness primarily, but of the disease of sin and its consequence: the wrath of God. Because of Christ, God is at peace with you. *Having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ* (Romans 5:1).

Calvin said: "When we behold the disfigurement of the Son of God, we need to reckon afresh that it is ourselves upon whom we gaze — for He stood in our place."

## 4. He Did It Willingly

> "...but committed Himself to Him who judges righteously." (1 Peter 2:23)

> "No one takes it from Me, but I lay it down of Myself." (John 10:18)

The night before the crucifixion, Jesus and His disciples were walking toward the upper room. The disciples were arguing about who would be greatest in the kingdom. John tells us: *Having loved His own who were in the world, He loved them to the end.* The very sin they were committing — pride — was about to send Him to the cross the next morning. And He looked at them and loved them.

Do not imagine that because He was God His suffering was less real. The drops of blood in Gethsemane were not theater. The cry from the cross — *My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?* — was not performance. He suffered. He suffered fully, really, infinitely. And He chose it — because without it, He could not have you.

## Application

If you are in Christ: *rejoice*. The price of your soul has been paid in full. There is nothing more to do. Cling to the cross and make it the center of everything you do. Live righteously — not to earn what Christ has already given you, but in response to it. Live *happily*, knowing that God's face shines upon you because He frowned upon His Son.

If you are not in Christ: come to Him now. He will not turn away anyone who comes. He has promised. But come today — tomorrow is not guaranteed.
